title: 🏥 Mean Time to Recovery [Edu] [v2]
description: DORA Pillar: Mean Time to Recovery — Measures how quickly a team can restore service after a production failure. Elite teams recover in under 1 hour.

list.name: environment
list.label: Environment
list.definition: SELECT DISTINCT environment FROM deployments ORDER BY environment
list.query: SELECT DISTINCT environment FROM deployments ORDER BY environment

panels.description: Data mode banner — shows whether this dashboard displays live GitHub data (green), synthetic seed data (amber), or demo data (blue).

targets.rawSql:
SELECT
  CASE mode
    WHEN 'live' THEN '📡 Live Data — ' || COALESCE(source_label, '')
    WHEN 'seed' THEN '🌱 Synthetic Seed Data — ' || COALESCE(source_label, '')
    WHEN 'demo' THEN '🎮 Demo Environment — ' || COALESCE(source_label, '')
    ELSE '❓ Unknown Mode'
  END AS "Data Source"
FROM data_mode
ORDER BY updated_at DESC LIMIT 1

options.content:
# 🔧 Mean Time to Recovery (MTTR)

**DORA Pillar: Stability**

## What it Measures
Mean Time to Recovery measures how long it takes to restore service after a production failure or incident. It is the second DORA *stability* metric and reflects your team's ability to detect, diagnose, and resolve production issues quickly.

## Why it Matters
Fast recovery reduces the impact of failures on users and the business. Teams with low MTTR can deploy with confidence, knowing that any issues will be resolved quickly. High MTTR often indicates gaps in monitoring, incident response processes, or deployment automation.

## How to Interpret
| Performance Level | Recovery Time | What it Means |
|---|---|---|
| 🟢 Elite | Less than 1 hour | Automated rollbacks, strong monitoring, fast incident response |
| 🟡 High | 1 hour to 1 day | Good processes with some manual intervention |
| 🟠 Medium | 1 day to 1 week | Slow diagnosis or complex recovery procedures |
| 🔴 Low | More than 1 week | Major gaps in monitoring or incident response |

**Incident Resolution Time** tracks issue-based incidents as a supplementary measure.

panels.description: Mean Time to Recovery — DORA stability pillar. Performance tiers and action guidance.

targets.rawSql:
WITH failure_windows AS (
  SELECT
    d.deployment_id,
    d.created_at AS failed_at,
    LEAD(d.created_at) OVER (PARTITION BY d.environment ORDER BY d.created_at) AS recovered_at
  FROM deployments d
  JOIN deployment_statuses ds ON ds.deployment_id = d.deployment_id
  WHERE ds.state = 'failure'
  AND ('$environment' = '__all__' OR d.environment = '$environment')
  AND d.created_at BETWEEN $__timeFrom() AND $__timeTo()
)
SELECT ROUND(PERCENTILE_CONT(0.5) WITHIN GROUP (
  ORDER BY EXTRACT(EPOCH FROM (recovered_at - failed_at)) / 3600.0
)::numeric, 1) AS "MTTR (hours)"
FROM failure_windows
WHERE recovered_at IS NOT NULL

options.content:
### 📡 GitHub API Source [→ docs](https://docs.github.com/en/rest/deployments/deployments)

`GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/deployments` + `GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/deployments/{id}/statuses`

### 🗄️ Database Table(s)

`deployments`, `deployment_statuses`

### 📐 How It's Calculated

**Proxy metric:** median time between a failed deployment and the next successful deployment in the same environment (SQL `LEAD()` window function). This measures deploy gap, not actual service restoration time.

### 🔍 SQL Query

```sql
WITH failure_windows AS (
  SELECT
    d.deployment_id,
    d.created_at AS failed_at,
    LEAD(d.created_at) OVER (PARTITION BY d.environment ORDER BY d.created_at) AS recovered_at
  FROM deployments d
  JOIN deployment_statuses ds ON ds.deployment_id = d.deployment_id
  WHERE ds.state = 'failure'
  AND ('$environment' = '__all__' OR d.environment = '$environment')
  AND d.created_at BETWEEN $__timeFrom() AND $__timeTo()
)
SELECT ROUND(PERCENTILE_CONT(0.5) WITHIN GROUP (
  ORDER BY EXTRACT(EPOCH FROM (recovered_at - failed_at)) / 3600.0
)::numeric, 1) AS "MTTR (hours)"
FROM failure_windows
WHERE recovered_at IS NOT NULL
```

targets.rawSql:
WITH failure_windows AS (
  SELECT
    d.deployment_id,
    d.created_at AS failed_at,
    LEAD(d.created_at) OVER (PARTITION BY d.environment ORDER BY d.created_at) AS recovered_at
  FROM deployments d
  JOIN deployment_statuses ds ON ds.deployment_id = d.deployment_id
  WHERE ds.state = 'failure'
  AND ('$environment' = '__all__' OR d.environment = '$environment')
  AND d.created_at BETWEEN $__timeFrom() AND $__timeTo()
)
SELECT ROUND(PERCENTILE_CONT(0.9) WITHIN GROUP (
  ORDER BY EXTRACT(EPOCH FROM (recovered_at - failed_at)) / 3600.0
)::numeric, 1) AS "P90 MTTR (hours)"
FROM failure_windows
WHERE recovered_at IS NOT NULL

options.content:
### 📡 GitHub API Source [→ docs](https://docs.github.com/en/rest/deployments/deployments)

`GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/deployments` + `/deployments/{id}/statuses`

### 🗄️ Database Table(s)

`deployments`, `deployment_statuses`

### 📐 How It's Calculated

90th-percentile of deploy-gap proxy using `PERCENTILE_CONT(0.9)`. Partitioned by environment using SQL `LEAD()` window function. A fix deployed to 'prod' does NOT close a failure on 'production' — environment names must match exactly.

### 🔍 SQL Query

```sql
WITH failure_windows AS (
  SELECT
    d.deployment_id,
    d.created_at AS failed_at,
    LEAD(d.created_at) OVER (PARTITION BY d.environment ORDER BY d.created_at) AS recovered_at
  FROM deployments d
  JOIN deployment_statuses ds ON ds.deployment_id = d.deployment_id
  WHERE ds.state = 'failure'
  AND ('$environment' = '__all__' OR d.environment = '$environment')
  AND d.created_at BETWEEN $__timeFrom() AND $__timeTo()
)
SELECT ROUND(PERCENTILE_CONT(0.9) WITHIN GROUP (
  ORDER BY EXTRACT(EPOCH FROM (recovered_at - failed_at)) / 3600.0
)::numeric, 1) AS "P90 MTTR (hours)"
FROM failure_windows
WHERE recovered_at IS NOT NULL
```

targets.rawSql:
SELECT COUNT(*) AS "Incidents Still Open"
FROM issues i
WHERE i.labels::jsonb @> '[{"name":"incident"}]'
  AND i.state = 'open'

options.content:
### 📡 GitHub API Source [→ docs](https://docs.github.com/en/rest/issues/issues)

`GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/issues?labels=incident&state=open`

### 🗄️ Database Table(s)

`issues`

### 📐 How It's Calculated

**All-time** count of open 'incident' issues — this stat ignores the Grafana time picker entirely. Includes issues that were never closed after resolution (common with stale incident tracking hygiene).

### 🔍 SQL Query

```sql
SELECT COUNT(*) AS "Incidents Still Open"
FROM issues i
WHERE i.labels::jsonb @> '[{"name":"incident"}]'
  AND i.state = 'open'
```

targets.rawSql:
WITH failure_windows AS (
  SELECT
    d.created_at AS failed_at,
    LEAD(d.created_at) OVER (PARTITION BY d.environment ORDER BY d.created_at) AS recovered_at
  FROM deployments d
  JOIN deployment_statuses ds ON ds.deployment_id = d.deployment_id
  WHERE ds.state = 'failure'
  AND ('$environment' = '__all__' OR d.environment = '$environment')
  AND d.created_at BETWEEN $__timeFrom() AND $__timeTo()
)
SELECT
  date_trunc('week', failed_at) AS time,
  ROUND(PERCENTILE_CONT(0.5) WITHIN GROUP (
    ORDER BY EXTRACT(EPOCH FROM (recovered_at - failed_at)) / 3600.0
  )::numeric, 1) AS "MTTR P50 (hours)",
  ROUND(PERCENTILE_CONT(0.9) WITHIN GROUP (
    ORDER BY EXTRACT(EPOCH FROM (recovered_at - failed_at)) / 3600.0
  )::numeric, 1) AS "MTTR P90 (hours)"
FROM failure_windows
WHERE recovered_at IS NOT NULL
GROUP BY 1
ORDER BY 1

options.content:
### 📡 GitHub API Source [→ docs](https://docs.github.com/en/rest/deployments/deployments)

`GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/deployments` + `/deployments/{id}/statuses`

### 🗄️ Database Table(s)

`deployments`, `deployment_statuses`

### 📐 How It's Calculated

Weekly median recovery time using the same `LEAD()` deploy-gap proxy. Weeks with no deployment failures appear as gaps — gaps are good news (no failures), not missing data. Low-failure weeks produce unstable medians (median of 1 event).

### 🔍 SQL Query

```sql
WITH failure_windows AS (
  SELECT
    d.created_at AS failed_at,
    LEAD(d.created_at) OVER (PARTITION BY d.environment ORDER BY d.created_at) AS recovered_at
  FROM deployments d
  JOIN deployment_statuses ds ON ds.deployment_id = d.deployment_id
  WHERE ds.state = 'failure'
  AND ('$environment' = '__all__' OR d.environment = '$environment')
  AND d.created_at BETWEEN $__timeFrom() AND $__timeTo()
)
SELECT
  date_trunc('week', failed_at) AS time,
  ROUND(PERCENTILE_CONT(0.5) WITHIN GROUP (
    ORDER BY EXTRACT(EPOCH FROM (recovered_at - failed_at)) / 3600.0
  )::numeric, 1) AS "MTTR P50 (hours)",
  ROUND(PERCENTILE_CONT(0.9) WITHIN GROUP (
    ORDER BY EXTRACT(EPOCH FROM (recovered_at - failed_at)) / 3600.0
  )::numeric, 1) AS "MTTR P90 (hours)"
FROM failure_windows
WHERE recovered_at IS NOT NULL
GROUP BY 1
ORDER BY 1
```

targets.rawSql:
SELECT
  date_trunc('week', i.created_at) AS time,
  ROUND(PERCENTILE_CONT(0.5) WITHIN GROUP (
    ORDER BY EXTRACT(EPOCH FROM (i.closed_at - i.created_at)) / 3600.0
  )::numeric, 1) AS "Issue Resolution Time P50 (hours)"
FROM issues i
WHERE i.labels::jsonb @> '[{"name":"incident"}]'
  AND i.closed_at IS NOT NULL
  AND i.created_at BETWEEN $__timeFrom() AND $__timeTo()
GROUP BY 1
ORDER BY 1

options.content:
### 📡 GitHub API Source [→ docs](https://docs.github.com/en/rest/issues/issues)

`GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/issues?labels=incident`

### 🗄️ Database Table(s)

`issues`

### 📐 How It's Calculated

Alternative MTTR signal: weekly median of `(closed_at - created_at)` for incident-labeled issues. Different from deployment-based MTTR — the two charts measure different things and will often disagree. Open issues are excluded entirely.

### 🔍 SQL Query

```sql
SELECT
  date_trunc('week', i.created_at) AS time,
  ROUND(PERCENTILE_CONT(0.5) WITHIN GROUP (
    ORDER BY EXTRACT(EPOCH FROM (i.closed_at - i.created_at)) / 3600.0
  )::numeric, 1) AS "Issue Resolution Time P50 (hours)"
FROM issues i
WHERE i.labels::jsonb @> '[{"name":"incident"}]'
  AND i.closed_at IS NOT NULL
  AND i.created_at BETWEEN $__timeFrom() AND $__timeTo()
GROUP BY 1
ORDER BY 1
```

targets.rawSql:
WITH failure_windows AS (
  SELECT
    d.deployment_id,
    d.environment,
    d.sha,
    d.creator_login,
    d.created_at AS failed_at,
    LEAD(d.created_at) OVER (PARTITION BY d.environment ORDER BY d.created_at) AS recovered_at
  FROM deployments d
  JOIN deployment_statuses ds ON ds.deployment_id = d.deployment_id
  WHERE ds.state = 'failure'
  AND ('$environment' = '__all__' OR d.environment = '$environment')
  AND d.created_at BETWEEN $__timeFrom() AND $__timeTo()
)
SELECT
  sha AS "SHA",
  environment AS "Environment",
  creator_login AS "Creator",
  failed_at AS "Failed At",
  recovered_at AS "Recovered At",
  ROUND(EXTRACT(EPOCH FROM (recovered_at - failed_at)) / 3600.0::numeric, 1) AS "Recovery Time (hours)"
FROM failure_windows
ORDER BY failed_at DESC
LIMIT 25

panels.title: Recent Failures with Recovery Time - Learning Guide

options.content:
### 📡 GitHub API Source [→ docs](https://docs.github.com/en/rest/deployments/deployments)

`GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/deployments` + `/deployments/{id}/statuses`

### 🗄️ Database Table(s)

`deployments`, `deployment_statuses`

### 📐 How It's Calculated

Table using `LEAD() OVER (PARTITION BY environment ORDER BY created_at)` to pair each failed deployment with the next deployment in the same environment. A NULL `recovered_at` means no subsequent deployment exists in that environment partition.

### 🔍 SQL Query

```sql
WITH failure_windows AS (
  SELECT
    d.deployment_id,
    d.environment,
    d.sha,
    d.creator_login,
    d.created_at AS failed_at,
    LEAD(d.created_at) OVER (PARTITION BY d.environment ORDER BY d.created_at) AS recovered_at
  FROM deployments d
  JOIN deployment_statuses ds ON ds.deployment_id = d.deployment_id
  WHERE ds.state = 'failure'
  AND ('$environment' = '__all__' OR d.environment = '$environment')
  AND d.created_at BETWEEN $__timeFrom() AND $__timeTo()
)
SELECT
  sha AS "SHA",
  environment AS "Environment",
  creator_login AS "Creator",
  failed_at AS "Failed At",
  recovered_at AS "Recovered At",
  ROUND(EXTRACT(EPOCH FROM (recovered_at - failed_at)) / 3600.0::numeric, 1) AS "Recovery Time (hours)"
FROM failure_windows
ORDER BY failed_at DESC
LIMIT 25
```
